title: Tenants' Guide to Eviction | Mass.gov
content:
# Tenants' Guide to Eviction | Mass.gov

A landlord cannot lock you out or throw you out of your apartment without a judge's order. This guide helps you understand the eviction process, from the notice to quit and mediating with your landlord to finding legal help and the eviction court process.

## What to Know First

Eviction is a process where a landlord tries to remove a tenant from the property that the tenant is renting. You will often see eviction cases called "summary process" or "summary process eviction" cases.

- A landlord cannot lock you out or throw you out of your apartment without a judge's order.
- In Mass., a landlord must send a tenant a Notice to Quit before filing an eviction case.
- Receiving a notice to quit does not mean you need to leave immediately.

### Getting Help

- Trying to solve problems with your landlord can help avoid eviction.
- Mediation is when a neutral party helps you and your landlord solve any disputes. Reach out for mediation help as quickly as possible.
- This is especially important if you are applying for RAFT (Residential Assistance for Families in Transition) because you need your landlord's cooperation to complete a RAFT application.

### Mediation and Legal Resources

- Massachusetts Community Mediation Centers offer free, pre-court mediation for landlord-tenant disputes and eviction cases.
- Legal Resource Finder connects you to legal aid programs, non-profits, government agencies, and court programs for free or low-cost assistance.
- The Commonwealth's Court Service Centers are available to all court users without legal representation. There are no income or immigration status requirements for the Centers.

## Eviction: The Step by Step Process

During each step in the eviction process, you may be able to resolve problems you and your landlord are having. If an eviction case goes to court, you may have to pay court fees. Eviction court cases can also affect your credit and housing histories.

1. Your landlord gives you a "notice to quit." The notice will say either 14 days (for non-payment of rent) or 30 days (for other reasons). This doesn't mean you have to move out — only a court order can force you to move out.
2. You can try to resolve your issues with your landlord. If the notice is because you're behind on rent, paying what you owe may prevent the eviction.
3. If you can't resolve things, your landlord may take you to court. You'll receive a "Summons and Complaint" from a constable or sheriff.
4. After you receive the Summons and Complaint, you may write a response and file it with the court.
5. You will be notified of the court date. If issues aren't resolved, you'll go to court in person.
6. In court, your case may be resolved by signing an agreement. If not, you go to trial where a judge (or jury) decides.
7. If the judge decides for the landlord, they may issue an "execution" — an order to move the eviction forward. You have 10 days to appeal.
8. To use the execution, the landlord must hire a sheriff or constable. They must give 2 days written notice before removing you and your belongings.
9. You may file a motion to stay the execution to ask for more time to move.

## A "Notice to Quit" is a Legal Document

A "notice to quit" is a legal document the landlord must give you to start the eviction process. It does not mean you have to leave. Only a court order can force you out.

### The 2 Most Common Notices

- **14-day notice to quit**: For nonpayment of rent. If you live in federally-assisted housing, you may get a 30-day notice.
- **30-day notice to quit**: For various reasons, including lease violation or landlord wants the unit back.

### What to Do if You Get a Notice to Quit

- Talk to your landlord to try to work things out.
- Try mediation — Community Mediation Centers offer free mediation services.
- If behind on rent, contact landlord to work out a payment plan.
- The RAFT program provides short-term emergency payments (up to $7,000 per year) for eligible families.
- Even if your landlord takes you to court, you can't be evicted if you have a pending RAFT application.
- Landlords are required by law to include a form listing rental assistance programs with a notice to quit for not paying rent.

### "No-Fault" Notice to Quit

Some notices are "no-fault" — the landlord wants to end the tenancy for reasons other than nonpayment (e.g., selling the property). A no-fault notice still means your landlord plans to take you to court.

## Preparing for an Eviction Court Case

Your landlord can file once the notice to quit date passes. They must:
- Fill out a Summons and Complaint form
- Hire a sheriff or constable to deliver it
- File it with the court

Landlords can file in District Court or Housing Court. Housing Courts are more familiar with housing laws.

### How to Answer a Summons and Complaint

File an answer — an official document you submit to the court for free. In it, explain your side and dispute any part you disagree with. Follow the instructions on the Summons and Complaint for how and when to file.

### How to Prepare for Court

Eviction court cases usually resolve in one of these ways:
- The case will be dismissed
- You'll settle with your landlord
- The case will be decided by the court

## After Your Eviction Trial: What Happens?

### Execution

If the judge decides in favor of the landlord, the tenant has 10 days from the date the clerk's office enters judgment to appeal.

### Stay of Execution

The court may postpone the eviction because it's not your fault or you can show you can't find a good place to live. If granted, you can stay up to 6 months. Elderly or disabled tenants can request a stay of up to a year.

### Appealing

You have 10 days to file an appeal. Before you can appeal, you may have to pay money owed (back rent). If you can't afford it, you can ask the court to waive it.

### If the Eviction Moves Forward

- 11 days after the court's final decision, the landlord can pay a sheriff or constable to move your things
- The sheriff/constable will give 2 days written notice. They can only move you Monday-Friday 9am-5pm (not holidays)
- Your landlord must execute the eviction within 3 months of the court decision
- If you pay all rent due before execution and the landlord accepts it, you can't be moved out

### If You Have to Move Out

- Your belongings go to a licensed storage facility
- If stored for 6 months without pay, the facility may sell them

### Eviction Records

- Evictions can stay on your housing record and affect your credit history
- You may be able to seal your eviction court record
- You can repair your credit by asking for a "satisfaction of judgment" form
